questions.question: What is the largest organ of the human body?
Which blood type is known as the "universal donor"?
How many ribs does the average adult human have?
What is the common name for the patella?
What substance gives skin and hair its pigment or color?
Which organ produces the hormone insulin?
What is the standard, average human body temperature in Fahrenheit rounded to the nearest tenth of a degree?
What acid is primarily found in the human stomach to aid digestion?
What color is human blood inside the body before it hits oxygen?
What is the name of the fluid-filled structure in the inner ear that looks like a snail shell and is essential for hearing?
Which type of blood vessels carry oxygenated blood away from the heart?
What is the medical term for the "voice box"?
Which human organ is capable of completely regenerating itself from just a small fraction of its original size?
What is the common name for the clavicle?
Which vitamin is naturally produced by the human body when exposed to sunlight?
An adult human skeleton has 206 bones. How many are in the hands and wrists?
What is the medical term for a professional who specializes in the study and treatment of the feet?
What classic medical tool was invented in 1816 by French physician René Laennec because he felt uncomfortable placing his ear directly on women's chests?
Which organ is responsible for filtering waste from the blood and producing urine?
What is the strongest and longest bone in the human body?
In the human eye, what is the name of the clear front surface that covers the iris and pupil, acting like a window?
Alexander Fleming famously discovered the first modern antibiotic in 1928. What was it?
What muscle, separating the chest cavity from the abdomen, is the primary muscle used in inhalation?
Jonas Salk famously refused to patent his 1955 medical breakthrough so that it could be distributed as widely and cheaply as possible. What disease did his vaccine target?
How many chambers are there in a human heart?
What type of tissue connects bones to other bones?
What type of tissue connects bones to muscle?
In what appendage is the stapes bone (the smallest bone in the human body) located?
Edward Jenner famously developed the world's first successful vaccine in 1796. Which disease did it target?
What is the only bone in the human body that is completely floating and not attached to any other bone?
What is the medical term for the "white of the eye"?
What element, with the chemical symbol Fe, is essential for forming hemoglobin in red blood cells?
What is the name of the medical condition characterized by a severe, sudden drop in bone density, making them fragile and brittle?
What is the medical term for high blood pressure?
True or False: Humans are born with more bones than they have as adults.
What does the acronym "MRI" stand for in medical imaging?
True or False: Your ears and your nose never stop growing throughout your entire life.
Which major organ is protected by the cranium?
What are the four front teeth on both the top and bottom jaw used for cutting food called?
What is the name of the colored part of the human eye that controls the size of the pupil?
In 1922, a 14-year-old boy in Toronto became the first human being to receive a successful injection of what life-saving hormone to treat diabetes?
What highly contagious disease, wiped out globally in 1980, remains the only human infectious disease to be completely eradicated through vaccination?
What is the specific name of the pigment found in red blood cells that uses iron to transport oxygen throughout the body?
What part of the eye, named after the Latin word for "net," contains the rods and cones that convert light into neural signals?
Humans have three types of blood cells: red, white, and a third type responsible for clotting blood to stop bleeding. What is this third type called?
Which organ filters all the blood leaving the stomach and intestines, breaking down nutrients and detoxifying chemicals?
What is the chemical name for the main sugar found in the blood, which serves as the body's primary source of energy?
Which organ in the human body consumes the largest share of our resting energy, burning roughly 20% of our daily calories?
What double-helix molecule, famously modeled by Watson, Crick, and Franklin in 1953, carries the genetic instructions for all living organisms?
Known as the powerhouse of the cell, what organelle is responsible for generating the chemical energy (ATP) our cells need to function?
How many pairs of chromosomes does a typical, healthy human cell contain?
In genetics, a trait that only expresses itself when an organism inherits two copies of the gene is known by what term?
Which muscle, located at the back of the upper arm, is primarily responsible for extending the elbow joint?
Viagra is famous for being a happy accident. What medical condition was Pfizer actually trying to treat when they discovered the drug's lucrative side effect?
What brand-name medication, introduced in 1987, became the first major FDA-approved Selective Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitor (SSRI) used to treat depression?
If you are taking a medication that acts as a statin (like Lipitor or Crestor), what specific substance in your blood is your doctor trying to lower?
What is the brand name of the portable, automated device used to deliver a life-saving dose of epinephrine during a severe allergic reaction?
What painkiller derives its name from the Greek god of dreams?
What class of life-saving medications derives its name from Greek words meaning "against life"?
What heavy metal, historically nicknamed "quicksilver," was used for centuries as a standard—and toxic—treatment for syphilis before antibiotics were discovered?
What do the letters "ICU" stand for in a hospital setting?
In pharmacy shorthand, what does the abbreviation "Rx" stand for?
What is the medical term for a condition where a patient has an abnormally low count of red blood cells, often causing fatigue and paleness?
If a doctor tells you a medication must be administered sublingually, where are you supposed to place it?
What does the abbreviation "OTC" mean when tracking down medications at a pharmacy?
What suffix is added to a medical term to indicate inflammation of an organ or tissue?
If someone is suffering an acute myocardial infarction, what common medical emergency is happening to them?
Which chronic metabolic disease is characterized by the body's inability to produce or properly use insulin, leading to elevated blood sugar levels?
What infectious disease, caused by a bacterium and historically known as "Consumption," famously ravaged Victorian society and typically targets the lungs?
What type of organism causes common conditions like athlete's foot, ringworm, and thrush?
What specific name is given to the medical field that studies the patterns, causes, and effects of health and disease?
What specific name is given to the medical oath of ethics historically taken by physicians, named after an ancient Greek doctor?

What English scientist is famously credited with discovering the three laws of motion, but also made significant contributions to early optics by sliding a blunt needle around the back of his own eye to study color perception?
What psychological condition describes a scenario where hostages develop a psychological bond or sympathy with their captors?
What medical term describes the process of sorting injured patients into categories based on the urgency of their need for care?
What Latin word, meaning "poison," gives us the modern word for sub-microscopic infectious agents that cannot replicate outside a host cell?
What is the medical term for a physician who specializes in diagnosing and treating cancers?
In hospital slang, what color code is universally used to broadcast an emergency announcement that a patient's heart has stopped or they have stopped breathing? Hint: "We have a code _______!"
What historical, terrifyingly named disease—caused by a vitamin C deficiency—led to bleeding gums, opening wounds, and loss of teeth for sailors on long voyages?
Which organ in the human body is the only one that lacks pain receptors, meaning it cannot actually feel pain directly?
What major gland, shaped like a butterfly and located in the front of the neck, controls your body's overall metabolism?
